course_info.title: Educational Technology Capstone
prerequisites: ED408 - Research Methods in Educational Technology

learning_objectives: Define the scope and deliverables of the capstone project
Identify a real-world educational technology problem to address
Draft a project proposal with clear objectives and timeline
readings: Examples of successful capstone projects from previous cohorts

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone project is the culmination of your educational technology education. You will identify a real-world problem, design a solution drawing on everything you have learned, build or implement it, evaluate its effectiveness, and present your work professionally.
key_concepts: Capstone scope: substantial enough to demonstrate mastery, focused enough to complete in one semester
Problem identification: work with a real client or community if possible
Project types: design and development, program evaluation, action research, implementation and evaluation
Deliverables: working product or implementation, research report, professional presentation
Project management: timeline, milestones, weekly progress reports
lecture_notes.demonstration: Proposal workshop: each student presents a 2-minute project pitch and receives structured feedback from peers and instructor on scope, feasibility, and significance

review.value: The most common capstone failure is overscoping. A focused, well-executed project is better than an ambitious, unfinished one. Define a minimum viable product and expand only if time permits.

The strongest capstones solve real problems for real clients: a school, a nonprofit, a training department, a community organization. Real-world constraints make the project more challenging and more valuable.

learning_objectives: Situate the capstone project within the relevant research literature
Select and justify a theoretical or conceptual framework
Write a focused literature review that supports the project design
readings: Boote, D. & Beile, P. - Scholars Before Researchers: On the Centrality of the Literature Review

lecture_notes.introduction: Your capstone project is not just a development project — it is a scholarly project. Grounding your work in theory and research distinguishes a professional educational technology product from a weekend hack.
key_concepts: Literature review purpose: establishing what is known, what is not known, and how your project fills the gap
Theoretical framework: the lens through which you design and evaluate (e.g., TPACK, CoI, UDL, SAM, ADDIE)
Synthesis vs. summary: connecting sources to build an argument
Citation management: Zotero, Mendeley, or similar tools
Annotated bibliography as a stepping stone to the literature review
lecture_notes.demonstration: Framework matching exercise: given five capstone project descriptions, identify the most appropriate theoretical framework for each and justify the choice

review.value: A theoretical framework is not decoration — it guides your design decisions and evaluation criteria. If your framework is Community of Inquiry, your design must address teaching, social, and cognitive presence, and your evaluation must measure them.
For a capstone, aim for 15-25 sources focused on your specific topic. Quality and relevance matter more than quantity. Every source should contribute to your argument.

learning_objectives: Develop a detailed project plan with methodology appropriate to the project type
Identify data collection and evaluation methods
Address ethical considerations and obtain necessary approvals
readings: McKenney, S. & Reeves, T. - Conducting Educational Design Research Ch. 5-7

lecture_notes.introduction: This session helps you finalize your project methodology. Whether you are doing design-based research, program evaluation, or implementation and evaluation, you need a clear, rigorous plan.
key_concepts: Design-based research methodology for development projects
Program evaluation methodology for implementation projects
Mixed methods: combining quantitative and qualitative data for rich understanding
Participant recruitment and ethical considerations
Data management plan: collection, storage, analysis, reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Methodology peer review: exchange project plans with a partner and provide structured feedback on methodology, feasibility, and alignment between research questions and methods

review.value: Your research questions drive your methodology. 'Does X improve learning?' requires quantitative comparison. 'How do teachers experience X?' requires qualitative interviews. 'How can X be designed to improve Y?' requires design-based research.
Collect data throughout the project, not just at the end. User testing, observation notes, and feedback during development are as valuable as final outcome measures.

learning_objectives: Demonstrate substantial progress on the capstone project
Present current work for peer and instructor feedback
Adjust project scope and timeline based on progress
readings: No assigned readings — focus on project development

lecture_notes.introduction: This session is a progress check and design review. Each student presents their current work, receives structured feedback, and adjusts their plan as needed. Honest progress reporting is essential.
key_concepts: Progress reporting: what is done, what is behind schedule, what has changed
Pivot vs. persist: when to adjust the plan based on what you are learning
Peer feedback: specific, constructive, actionable
Time management: working backward from the deadline
Documentation: keeping a project journal for the final report
lecture_notes.demonstration: Progress presentations: each student gives a 5-minute presentation of current work with a 3-minute feedback discussion

review.value: Reporting that everything is 'fine' when you are behind schedule helps no one. Early identification of problems allows for course correction. Late identification causes crisis.
If you are behind schedule, reduce scope to your minimum viable product rather than rushing to produce something incomplete. Quality over quantity.

learning_objectives: Conduct formative evaluation of the capstone product or implementation
Collect and analyze user feedback systematically
Revise the project based on evaluation findings
readings: Nielsen, J. - Usability Engineering Ch. 6 (Usability Testing)
Tessmer, M. - Planning and Conducting Formative Evaluations Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Your capstone project must include evidence from real users. This session prepares you for conducting formative evaluation and using the results to improve your work.
key_concepts: Formative evaluation methods: expert review, one-to-one tryout, small group evaluation
Think-aloud protocol: users narrate their experience while using your product
Usability metrics: task completion, time-on-task, error rate, satisfaction
Feedback analysis: identifying patterns across users
Revision planning: prioritizing changes by impact and feasibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formative evaluation practice: conduct a brief think-aloud evaluation of a partner's capstone project and provide structured feedback

review.value: Nielsen's research shows that 5 users reveal approximately 80% of usability problems. For the capstone, aim for 3-5 formative evaluation participants.
Not all feedback requires changes. Prioritize by: (1) how many users experienced the issue, (2) how severely it impacts learning, and (3) how feasible the fix is within the remaining timeline.

learning_objectives: Present the capstone project professionally to a panel audience
Defend design decisions and evaluation findings under questioning
Reflect on the capstone experience and professional growth

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone presentation is your professional showcase. You will present your project to a panel of faculty and peers, demonstrate your product, discuss your findings, and defend your decisions. This simulates a real-world professional presentation.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: problem, approach, product, evaluation, findings, reflection
Product demonstration: live demo with backup plan
Handling questions: listen, acknowledge, respond with evidence
Professional presentation skills: pacing, eye contact, visual aids
Portfolio documentation: documenting the capstone for your professional portfolio
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presentation rehearsal: practice the opening 3 minutes of your presentation and receive feedback on delivery, clarity, and pacing

review.value: Live demos fail. Have screenshots, a recorded video walkthrough, or a slideshow backup ready. Never rely solely on a live demo with internet-dependent tools.
A defense is not an attack. Panelists ask questions to understand your thinking and help you articulate your reasoning. Respond with evidence and honesty, including acknowledging limitations.

major_assignments.title: Capstone Project: Design, Implementation, and Evaluation
major_assignments.description: Identify a real-world educational technology problem, design and build a solution grounded in theory and research, evaluate it with real users, and present your work professionally. The project must include a working product or implementation, a research report documenting the full process, and a professional presentation with defense.
objectives: Apply the full range of educational technology knowledge and skills to a real-world problem
Design, build, and evaluate a technology-based educational solution
Document the project with scholarly rigor and professional quality
Present and defend the work to a professional audience
deliverables: Working product, prototype, or documented implementation
Research report (5,000-7,000 words) including literature review, methodology, findings, and discussion
Professional presentation (20 minutes) with product demonstration
Project portfolio including all artifacts, data, and documentation

criteria: Design decisions grounded in theory and research
Product demonstrates technical competence and instructional quality
Findings honestly reported with limitations acknowledged

project_milestones.description: Proposal, literature review, methodology, progress reports, and draft submissions

final_product.description: Working product or implementation with documentation

research_report.description: Scholarly report documenting the full capstone process and findings

presentation_and_defense.description: Professional presentation with product demonstration and panel Q&A

professionalism.description: Weekly progress reports, peer feedback contributions, and professional conduct

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at all sessions is mandatory. Progress reviews, peer feedback, and presentations cannot be rescheduled. More than 1 unexcused absence may affect the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late milestones delay peer feedback and reduce the quality of the final product. 10% penalty per day, maximum 3 days.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is required throughout the semester. The capstone project itself must be individually produced unless a team project is approved in advance.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly attribute all external code, content, and tools. Data must be ethically collected and honestly reported. Fabrication results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Varies by project. Students are responsible for identifying and securing the technology needed for their specific capstone project. Institutional lab resources are available.
